content:
# Judges Chapter 9 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_277.jpg, JDG_page_278.jpg, JDG_page_279.jpg

### Abimelech's Rise and Fall

1. One day Abimelech son of Gideon went to Shechem to visit his uncles — his mother's brothers. He said to them and to his mother's entire clan,

2. "Ask the leading citizens of Shechem whether they would rather be ruled by all seventy of Gideon's sons or by one man. And remember that I am your own flesh and blood!"

3. So Abimelech's uncles spoke on his behalf to all the citizens of Shechem, and they decided in his favor. "After all, he is our relative," they said.

4. They gave him seventy silver coins from the temple of Baal-berith, which he used to hire a gang of reckless troublemakers who agreed to follow him.

5. He went to his father's home in Ophrah and murdered all seventy of his half-brothers, the sons of Gideon, on a single stone. But the youngest brother, Jotham, escaped because he hid.

6. Then all the leading citizens of Shechem and Beth-millo gathered at the oak beside the pillar in Shechem and crowned Abimelech king.

7. When Jotham heard about this, he climbed to the top of Mount Gerizim and shouted down to them, "Listen to me, citizens of Shechem, so that God may listen to you!

8. Once upon a time the trees decided to choose a king. First they said to the olive tree, 'Be our king!'

9. But the olive tree refused, saying, 'Should I give up my rich oil, by which both God and people are honored, just to sway back and forth over the trees?'

10. Then they said to the fig tree, 'You be our king!'

11. But the fig tree also refused, saying, 'Should I give up my sweet fruit just to sway back and forth over the trees?'

12. Then they said to the grapevine, 'You be our king!'

13. But the grapevine replied, 'Should I give up my wine, which cheers both God and people, just to sway back and forth over the trees?'

14. Then all the trees finally said to the thornbush, 'Come, you be our king!'

15. And the thornbush replied, 'If you truly want to make me your king, come and take shelter under my shade. But if not, let fire come out of me and burn up even the cedars of Lebanon!'"

16. "Now then," Jotham continued, "was it an honest and honorable thing that you made Abimelech your king? Have you treated Gideon and his family fairly? Have you treated him as he deserved?

17. My father fought for you and risked his life to rescue you from the Midianites.

18. But today you have turned against my father's family, killing his seventy sons on a single stone. And you have made Abimelech — the son of his slave woman — king over the citizens of Shechem, just because he is your relative.

19. If you have acted with honesty and integrity toward Gideon and his descendants today, then may you find joy in Abimelech, and may he find joy in you.

20. But if you have not, then may fire come from Abimelech and devour the citizens of Shechem and Beth-millo; and may fire come from the citizens of Shechem and Beth-millo and devour Abimelech!"

21. Then Jotham fled. He went to Beer and lived there because he was afraid of his brother Abimelech.

22. After Abimelech had ruled over Israel for three years,

23. God stirred up trouble between Abimelech and the citizens of Shechem, and they turned against him.

24. God did this to avenge the murder of Gideon's seventy sons and to punish Abimelech, who killed them, and the citizens of Shechem, who had helped him.

25. The citizens of Shechem set an ambush for Abimelech on the hilltops and robbed everyone who passed by. But Abimelech was told about their biblplot.

26. Then Gaal son of Ebed moved to Shechem with his brothers, and the citizens of Shechem put their trust in him.

27. They went out to the fields, harvested their grapes, trod them in the winepresses, and held a festival in the temple of their god. They ate and drank and cursed Abimelech.

28. "Who is Abimelech," shouted Gaal, "and why should we be his servants? He's merely the son of Gideon, and Zebul is merely his deputy. We should serve the true men of Shechem — the descendants of Hamor, Shechem's founder. Why should we serve Abimelech?

29. If I were in charge of this people, I would get rid of Abimelech! I would say to him, 'Get your army together, and come out and fight!'"

30. But when Zebul, the governor of the city, heard what Gaal was saying, he was furious.

31. He secretly sent messengers to Abimelech, telling him, "Gaal son of Ebed and his brothers have come to Shechem, and they are stirring up the city against you.

32. Come by night with your men and hide in the fields.

33. In the morning, as soon as the sun is up, attack the city. When Gaal and his men come out against you, you can do whatever you want to them."

34. So Abimelech and his men marched by night and split into four companies to lie in ambush near Shechem.

35. Gaal was standing at the city gate when Abimelech and his men came out of hiding.

36. When Gaal saw them, he said to Zebul, "Look, there are people coming down from the hilltops!" Zebul replied, "It's just the shadows on the hills. You're seeing things."

37. But Gaal said again, "No, look! There are people coming down from the central hill, and another group is coming from the direction of the Diviners' Oak!"

38. Then Zebul turned on him and demanded, "Now where is that big mouth of yours? You're the one who said, 'Who is Abimelech, and why should we be his servants?' The men you despised are right here. So go out and fight them!"

39. So Gaal led the citizens of Shechem into battle against Abimelech.

40. But Abimelech chased him, and Gaal and many of his men fell wounded before they could retreat to the gate.

41. Abimelech returned to Arumah, and Zebul drove Gaal and his relatives out of Shechem.

42. The next day the people of Shechem went out to the fields. When Abimelech heard about it,

43. he divided his men into three groups and set an ambush in the fields. When the people came out of the city, he attacked and killed them.

44. Abimelech and his group rushed to the city gate to block the escape route, while the other two groups struck down the people in the fields.

45. The battle continued all day before Abimelech finally captured the city. He killed everyone in it, tore it down, and scattered salt over it.

46. When the leading citizens who lived in the tower of Shechem heard what had happened, they ran to the stronghold of the temple of El-berith.

47. When Abimelech learned that they were all together,

48. he led his forces to Mount Zalmon. He took an axe, cut some branches, and put them on his shoulder. "Quick! Do what I'm doing!" he told his men.

49. So each of them cut branches and followed Abimelech. They piled the branches against the walls of the stronghold and set them on fire. So all the people in the tower of Shechem died — about 1,000 men and women.

50. Then Abimelech attacked the town of Thebez and captured it.

51. But there was a strong tower inside the town, and all the men, women, and leading citizens fled to it. They locked themselves in and climbed up to the roof.

52. Abimelech came to attack the tower. But as he prepared to set fire to the entrance,

53. a woman on the roof dropped a millstone on his head and cracked his skull.

54. He quickly called to his young armor bearer, "Draw your sword and kill me! Don't let them say a woman killed me!" So the young man ran him through with his sword, and he died.

55. When the Israelites saw that Abimelech was dead, they all went home.

56. In this way God punished Abimelech for the evil he had done against his father by murdering his seventy brothers.

57. God also punished the men of Shechem for their evil. And so the curse of Jotham son of Gideon came true.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Jotham's Fable (vv.8-15):** This brilliant political satire is the only fable in the Hebrew Bible. Its message is timeless: productive people don't seek power — they're too busy being useful. Only the thornbush (worthless, combustible, casting almost no shade) is eager to rule. And its first act as "king" is to threaten the very trees it rules: "If you don't shelter under me, I'll burn you — even the cedars of Lebanon." The thornbush is Abimelech perfectly.

**The millstone (v.53):** The irony is exquisite. Abimelech, who killed seventy men on one stone (v.5), is himself killed by one stone — an upper millstone, a domestic grinding tool dropped by an unnamed woman from a rooftop. His desperate final words — "Don't let them say a woman killed me!" — are futile. Two hundred years later, the incident is still remembered (2 Samuel 11:21).

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
